chunk_summary: This section discusses the use of urinary iodine concentration (UIC) as a population-level indicator of iodine status, its limitations for individual diagnosis, and the role of thyroid function tests in clinical care.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of median urinary iodine concentration (UIC) in assessing population iodine status.
Recognize the limitations of single-sample UIC for individual iodine diagnosis.
Identify appropriate diagnostic tests for thyroid dysfunction in individual care.
Analyze the impact of iodized salt programs on iodine deficiency and thyroid health.
key_facts: Median UIC of 100–199 µg/L indicates adequate iodine intake for populations.
Single-sample UIC has high variability and is unreliable for individual diagnosis.
Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4) are essential for individual thyroid evaluation.
Goiter assessment post-iodization may involve autoimmune thyroiditis or other causes.
India shows improved iodized salt coverage but with state-level variations in deficiency and excess.
reference_quote: The preferred indicator for iodine status at a population level is median urinary iodine concentration (UIC), because most absorbed iodine is excreted in urine and UIC reflects recent intake.
document_key_topics: Thyroid function tests for individual care
Impact of iodized salt programs on iodine nutrition
